did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper states that it previously reported using machine learning to differentiate between BCCs and normal skin with high sensitivity and specificity, utilizing pixel intensity versus depth plot parameters from OCT images. While the current study focuses on comparing VOCT and histopathology, it explicitly references the use of machine learning as part of the method for obtaining diagnostic results in dermatology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a pilot study comparing histopathology, OCT images, and VOCT measurements on two specific BCC lesions. It includes specific Methods (image collection, processing), Results (observations of nodules, margins, vibrational spectra), and Conclusions derived from this new data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Noninvasive Screening of Basal Cell Carcinomas: A Comparison of the Structure and Physical Properties of Large and Small Nodular Lesions Using Vibrational OCT and Histopathology
brief_summary: This pilot study compared the structure and physical properties of small and large nodular basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) using histopathology, optical coherence tomography (OCT), and vibrational optical coherence tomography (VOCT). The researchers analyzed two specific BCC lesions, examining OCT images broken into subchannels and mechanovibrational spectra to identify resonant frequencies of tissue components like collagen and cancer-associated fibroblasts. Results indicated that while both lesion sizes exhibited similar morphologies with palisading cells, the large lesion showed increased fibrotic peaks and lacked clean edges compared to the small lesion. The study demonstrated that VOCT can noninvasively identify BCC nodules and their physical properties through resonant frequency measurements. The authors conclude that VOCT serves as a useful adjunct to visual inspection and dermoscopy for screening BCCs, particularly in telemedicine contexts where dermatologists are scarce.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on two specific nodular basal cell carcinoma lesions (from patient 34 and patient 39) collected as part of a larger 50-lesion study. The paper compares the structure and physical properties of these small and large nodular BCCs using histopathology, OCT, and VOCT.
Used as a baseline for comparison in OCT imaging and VOCT measurements (e.g., pixel intensity vs depth plots, resonant frequencies) to distinguish cancerous tissue from healthy tissue.

Mentioned in the Introduction and Discussion as another type of nonmelanoma skin cancer and in the context of previous VOCT studies showing similar biophysical properties (resonant frequency peaks) to BCCs, but not studied in the current experiments.

Mentioned in the Discussion as another carcinoma type where new resonant frequency peaks were found in previous VOCT studies, suggesting similar biophysical properties to BCCs, but not studied in the current experiments.

Mentioned in the Discussion as a category that needs to be distinguished from cancerous lesions using new techniques, and referenced in the context of previous VOCT studies differentiating benign from cancerous lesions.

all_task_types.target: Physical properties and structural characteristics of tissue (resonant frequencies, pixel intensity, fibrosis levels).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a pilot study comparing histopathology, OCT images, and VOCT measurements to identify the location of BCC nodules and characterize their physical properties (e.g., resonant frequencies of collagen and cancer-associated fibroblasts). While the abstract mentions using machine learning in a previous study to differentiate BCCs from normal skin (which would be classification), the current study's methodology focuses on physical measurement and comparison of spectral and structural features rather than training a model for discrete class prediction or continuous value regression. The task is best described as physical property characterization/screening via vibrational spectroscopy.

datasets_used.name: Rutgers Center for Dermatology Clinical Cohort (Subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of 2 nodular basal cell carcinoma (BCC) lesions (one small, one large) from patients 34 and 39, collected as part of a larger 50-lesion study previously published [5]. Images and VOCT data were collected in vivo on intact skin and in vitro on excised biopsies using a modified Lumedica OQ Labscope 2.0. The study also utilized data from normal skin for comparison.

per_class.Normal skin: Referenced as comparator, specific count not reported for this subset

use.testing: Used for comparative analysis of structure and physical properties (resonant frequencies, pixel intensity) between small and large nodular BCCs and normal skin.
use.other: The larger 50-lesion study from which these 2 lesions were drawn was previously used to train/validate a machine learning model for differentiating BCCs from normal skin with 93.7% sensitivity and 100% specificity, but this specific paper focuses on pilot comparison of the 2 lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study is a pilot case series comparing structural and physical properties of two specific BCC lesions against normal skin using descriptive observations and spectral analysis.

Small Nodular BCC (Patient 34).histopathology: Circular nodule with palisading cells; clear margins lacking rete ridges; some inflammatory cells present.
Small Nodular BCC (Patient 34).oct_subchannels: Green subchannel showed sparse reflection and decreased intensity compared to normal skin. Blue and red subchannels showed differences in components. Hyporeflective region near surface disappeared in blue subchannel.
Small Nodular BCC (Patient 34).voct_resonant_frequencies: Large resonant frequency peaks at approximately 60 Hz (normal cells), 80 Hz (cancer-associated fibroblasts), 100 Hz (papillary dermal collagen), and 260 Hz (fibrotic tissue). Increased peak sizes at 80 and 260 Hz compared to normal skin suggest cellular proliferation and fibrotic tissue deposition.
Small Nodular BCC (Patient 34).pixel_intensity: Decreased height of the green subchannel compared to normal skin.
Small Nodular BCC (Patient 34).vibrational_behavior: Disappearance of green, blue, and red subchannel images at vibrational frequencies of 60 and 70 Hz. Images at 50 and 80 Hz appeared similar.

Large Nodular BCC (Patient 39).histopathology: Nodule surrounded by tissue with increased fibrotic peaks; edges not clean; lack of hyporeflective regions in blue channel.
Large Nodular BCC (Patient 39).oct_subchannels: Green subchannel image ends were of low pixel intensity. Circled regions appeared different from rest of cancerous nodules but did not look like normal skin. Blue channel lacked hyporeflective regions seen in normal skin.
Large Nodular BCC (Patient 39).voct_resonant_frequencies: Absence of large 50 Hz and 80 Hz peaks. Very large peaks starting at 130 to 280 Hz. Peaks at 130-140 Hz suggest small blood vessels present. Peaks at 180 through 280 Hz suggest large amount of fibrotic tissue. Reduced peak heights at 50 and 80 Hz compared to small BCC.
Large Nodular BCC (Patient 39).pixel_intensity: Reduced pixel intensity for the green subchannel compared to normal skin and the small nodular BCC. Green subchannel peak height was only a small fraction of that of normal skin.
Large Nodular BCC (Patient 39).vibrational_behavior: Circled areas of the lesion became faint at vibrational frequencies of 60 and 70 Hz. Remainder of lesion also very faint at 60 and 70 Hz, suggesting edges are not 'clean'.

Normal Skin (Comparator).voct_resonant_frequencies: Reference baseline for comparison; specific peak values not explicitly listed for normal skin in this subset, but used as baseline for 'reduced' or 'increased' comparisons in BCCs.
Normal Skin (Comparator).oct_features: Exhibited hyporeflective regions in the blue channel (absent in large BCC).

Previous Study (Reference [5] - 50 Lesio.task: Differentiation between BCCs and normal skin using machine learning on pixel intensity versus depth plot parameters.

architecture_details.description: The paper references a previous study [5] where machine learning was used to differentiate between BCCs and normal skin. The input features for that model were 'pixel intensity versus depth plot parameters' (specifically maximum pixel height and width at half height) derived from OCT images. The current study does not report a specific AI architecture, model family, or neural network details, focusing instead on physical measurements via VOCT and OCT.

training_details.note: The current paper does not report training details, loss functions, optimizers, or hyperparameters for any AI model.

preprocessing_techniques: OCT grayscale scans were color-coded using ImageJ software to generate green, blue, and red subchannel images based on pixel intensity distribution (low, medium, high) via a lookup table.
Pixel intensity versus depth plots were generated by scanning grayscale OCT images parallel to the surface.
Raw OCT data were processed using MATLAB R2024b and ImageJ software 1.5.
conclusions: VOCT is a noninvasive technique that can be used to screen for basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) in conjunction with dermoscopy and visual inspection.
It is possible to identify the location of BCC nodules noninvasively using VOCT.
The ability to noninvasively identify nodular BCCs makes VOCT a useful adjunct to visual inspection and dermoscopy for identifying cancerous lesions in the clinic.
Since VOCT can be operated remotely, it can serve as a noninvasive screening tool to be used by general practitioners in areas where dermatologists are in short supply.

Both small and large nodular BCCs are characterized by nodules clearly seen by histopathology that can also be seen noninvasively by OCT imaging and characterized by measurements of the resonant frequencies of tissue components.
While the edges of small BCC lesions are free of cancer, the edges of large BCC lesions do not appear clean by both histopathology and VOCT measurements.

The mechanovibrational spectrum of large BCC lesions appears to have an increased amount of large fibrotic peaks with decreased vibrations for normal cells and cancer-associated fibroblasts compared to smaller nodules.
Collagen deposition and orientation may differ during the growth of different nodular BCCs.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot case series with an extremely limited sample size (n=2 lesions from two patients), which severely restricts statistical power and generalizability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; conclusions are based on descriptive observations of physical properties.
The dataset is drawn from a larger 50-lesion study but only two specific lesions (one small, one large nodular BCC) were analyzed in this paper, creating a potential selection bias.
External validation is lacking; the study relies on in vivo and in vitro data from a single center (Rutgers Center for Dermatology) using a specific device (modified Lumedica OQ Labscope 2.0).

The paper references previous machine learning results (93.7% sensitivity, 100% specificity) from a different study [5] but does not report any new model training or validation metrics for the current VOCT/histopathology comparison.
The comparison between OCT/VOCT findings and histopathology is limited to two excised biopsies, preventing robust correlation analysis across diverse lesion types or stages.

The study focuses on nodular BCCs; results may not apply to other BCC subtypes (e.g., superficial, infiltrative, morpheaform) or other skin cancers like SCC or melanoma, despite mentions in the discussion.

Data leakage risk is low as the current paper does not train models, but the reliance on previously published data for context requires careful distinction between the current pilot findings and prior ML performance claims.
